Oamenii efectiv au devenit uluitor de proști, așa că pentru cineva din 2026 Idiocracy nu mai are nimic SF în el: totul în film pare banal, e ca și cum te-ai […] The post Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Om cu principii solide, Nicușor Dan rămâne un mediator indiferent de situația în care se regăsește. Chiar și azi, când Sorin Grindeanu l-a trimis după țigări, președintele și-a demonstrat încă o dată abilitățile de mediator și, în locul unui refuz categoric, a ales să-i medieze liderului PSD pachetul de țigări cerut. Nicușor Dan și-a mediat […] The post Președinte-mediator! Grindeanu l-a trimis pe Nicușor să-i medieze un pachet de țigări appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 S-a aflat adevărata identitate a şoferului lui Simion. Era Grindeanu!
romania.summary: Mai ceva ca SPP-istul lui Nicuşor Dan, misteriosul şofer al liderului AUR George Simion a stârnit constant imaginaţia a mii de bărbaţi! Astăzi, însă, identitatea acestuia ieşit la iveală – şi, culmea, e de la un partid concurent. „În tot acest timp, şoferul lui Simion era Grindeanu! Oau! Mă încearcă nişte sentimente tare dubioase acum… […] The post S-a aflat adevărata identitate a şoferului lui Simion. Era Grindeanu! appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

romania.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
romania.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
romania.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

medical.title: Why your brain turns against you during arguments, and what to do about it
medical.summary: My ex once told me, mid-argument, that I was the most unempathetic person he'd ever met. It was a low blow. I'm a clinical psychologist. Empathy is literally my job.

medical.title: Oxidative stress slows down the brain, but the brake can be released again
medical.summary: As we age, the brain undergoes various changes. Neurons function less efficiently, and key regulatory systems within the cells become unbalanced. Protein quality control, which ensures that proteins are properly processed, recycled, or broken down, is particularly affected.

medical.title: Patients in low-income neighborhoods are less likely to receive cancer screenings
medical.summary: People living in areas with fewer resources are less likely to receive cancer screenings from federal clinics, according to a recent study from the University of Georgia published in the Journal of General Internal Medicine.

medical.title: Choosing safer diabetes medications for older adults
medical.summary: Older adults with type 2 diabetes face a difficult trade-off: they are among the most vulnerable to medication-related harms yet are often underrepresented in the clinical trials that guide treatment decisions. A new study led by Yuan Lu, ScD, helps address this gap by providing large-scale, real-world evidence about the safety of commonly used diabetes medications. The study was co-led by Marc Suchard, Ph.D., of the University of California, Los Angeles, who also served as the study's corresponding author.

medical.title: Magic tricks can reduce stress, pain and anxiety for children during vaccinations
medical.summary: Injections can be a source of stress and anxiety for children. The Willem-Alexander Children's Hospital is exploring ways to improve the experience. Together with illusionist Victor Mids and researchers from Leiden, they have shown that magic can help. The work is published in the journal eClinicalMedicine.

medical.title: Researchers deepen understanding of molecular pathways responsible for airway inflammation in asthma
medical.summary: Johns Hopkins Medicine researchers say they have identified molecular pathways controlled by the protein RhoA in club cells—a specialized population of epithelial cells that exclusively lines the inner and outer surfaces of the lung's small airways, with known roles in immune regulation and airway repair—during allergen-induced airway inflammation. Through a series of translational mouse and cell culture experiments, the research team has demonstrated how RhoA can be selectively targeted to potentially prevent asthma-related complications.

medical.title: Common genetic marker may guide new treatment for acute leukemia
medical.summary: A genetic alteration that is already routinely analyzed in patients with acute myeloid leukemia can be used to identify patients who respond to a new targeted therapy, according to a study published in the journal Discover Oncology. Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is an aggressive blood cancer in which treatment outcomes vary widely between patients. In the new study, the researchers have identified a way to better select which patients may benefit from a drug that blocks the enzyme DCPS.

medical.title: Study reveals century-long cycles in US suicide rates and a long-term crisis among youth
medical.summary: Suicide rates in the United States follow striking, decades-long cycles likely shaped by broad social forces, according to a major new study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS). But beneath that long-term rise-and-fall pattern, researchers uncovered a deeply concerning and persistent trend: suicide risk among young people has been rising steadily for more than a half century, affecting each new generation earlier in life.

medical.title: A routine virus can slow breast cancer spread to the lungs, offering hidden protective power
medical.summary: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), mostly infects the lungs, nose, throat, and respiratory tract, and can cause illness ranging from mild cold and fever-like symptoms to severe pneumonia and bronchitis. A recent study has found that having a respiratory infection can act as a shield against the spread of cancer cells.

medical.title: Phone-based education improves inhaler use in people with COPD
medical.summary: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), which includes emphysema and chronic bronchitis, affects more than 30 million Americans and is the fourth leading cause of death worldwide. Many people with COPD use inhaler-based medications as part of their treatment plan; however, research has shown that most people do not use these medications correctly, which can reduce effectiveness and result in more frequent exacerbations.

medical.title: Tiny daily movements key to preventing type 2 diabetes
medical.summary: Small habits like walking up flights of stairs or carrying your shopping instead of using a trolley could be the key to preventing Type 2 Diabetes, according to world-first research led by Monash University. In the first study in the world to highlight a link between short bursts of physical activity and lower risk of developing Type 2 Diabetes, published in Diabetes Care, researchers found less than four minutes a day of short bursts of moderate to intense activity could reduce a person's risk of developing the condition by more than a third.

medical.title: Scientists discover why bread can cause weight gain without extra calories
medical.summary: Bread and other carbohydrate staples may be doing more than just filling plates—they could be quietly reshaping metabolism. In a surprising twist, researchers found that mice strongly preferred carbs like bread, rice, and wheat, abandoning their regular diet entirely. Even without eating more calories, they gained weight and body fat, not because they overate, but because their bodies burned less energy.

medical.title: Blocking a single protein supercharges the immune system against cancer
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered a way to supercharge the immune system’s T cells by blocking a protein called Ant2, forcing the cells to rewire how they generate energy. This shift makes them more powerful, resilient, and effective at finding and destroying cancer cells.

medical.title: Lonely people have worse memory but don’t decline faster, study finds
medical.summary: Loneliness may quietly affect how well older adults remember things—but it might not be speeding up mental decline after all. A large European study tracking over 10,000 people for seven years found that those who felt lonelier started off with weaker memory, yet their memory didn’t deteriorate any faster than those who felt more socially connected. The findings challenge the idea that loneliness directly accelerates cognitive decline or dementia, suggesting instead that it impacts baseline brain performance.

medical.title: This strange “pearling” motion inside cells could change how we understand disease
medical.summary: Mitochondria don’t just generate energy—they also carefully organize their own DNA in a surprisingly elegant way. Scientists have discovered that a long-overlooked phenomenon called “mitochondrial pearling,” where mitochondria briefly form bead-like shapes, helps evenly space clusters of mitochondrial DNA.

medical.title: The people you live with could be changing your gut bacteria
medical.summary: Spending time with close companions might do more than strengthen bonds—it could also reshape your gut bacteria. In a study of island birds, those with stronger social ties shared more gut microbes, especially types that require direct contact to spread. This suggests that social interaction itself—not just shared space—drives microbial exchange. The same process may be happening in human households through everyday closeness.

science.title: International partners boost peace agreement success, study finds
science.summary: Over the past 50 years, nearly 4 in 10 peace agreements have failed within five years of signing. New research shows that when international partners help implement an accord, the odds improve—and the deeper their engagement, the better.

science.title: Observing exotic quasiparticle states in kagome superconductor CsV₃Sb₅
science.summary: A research team led by Prof. Hao Ning of the Hefei Institutes of Physical Science of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, in collaboration with Anhui University and the University of Science and Technology of China, has identified two distinct types of unusual low-energy quasiparticle states in the kagome superconductor CsV3Sb5 using single-atom impurities as local "quantum probes" combined with scanning tunneling spectroscopy.

science.title: Designing in situ power stations for future Mars missions
science.summary: You're in the lab analyzing Martian regolith samples within your cozy Mars habitat serving on the fifth human mission to Mars. The power within the habitat has been flowing flawlessly thanks to the MARS-MES (Mars Atmospheric Resource & Multimodal Energy System), including the general habitat lighting, science lab, sleeping quarters, exercise equipment, the virtual reality headsets the crew use for rest & relaxation, oxygen and fuel generation, and water. All this from converting the Martian atmosphere into workable electricity.

science.title: Cities rethink beekeeping as honeybee boom may strain wild bees
science.summary: The rising popularity of urban beekeeping has raised concerns about honeybee well-being and the impact they might have on wild bee populations in cities. A collaborative study by beekeepers, political stakeholders and research institutions, including researchers from the Technical University of Munich (TUM) has resulted in the "Urban Bee Concept," which includes measures to foster co-existence between honeybees and wild bees in cities. The study is published in the journal People and Nature.

science.title: Researchers detect microplastics in fish larvae shortly after hatching
science.summary: Microplastics are now widely distributed throughout the environment—in water, in the air, in the soil and even inside living organisms, including marine life. However, most studies to date have focused on adult fish, including those used for human consumption. The history of these microplastics, however, remained unknown. It was not clear exactly when this contamination begins throughout the life cycle.

science.title: The most common planets in the galaxy don't appear around the most common stars, TESS observations suggest
science.summary: Astronomers now estimate there is at least one planet for every star in our galaxy. These worlds, called exoplanets, are planets that orbit stars outside our solar system. But new research from McMaster University reveals a surprising twist: the most common planets in our galaxy don't exist around the most common stars.

science.title: Behavioral safety assessment may quantify the 'ability to see risk' to prevent industrial accidents
science.summary: Seoul National University College of Engineering announced that a research team has developed a behavior-based safety assessment system capable of measuring construction workers' ability to perceive risks and respond appropriately to them. The team was led by Prof. Changbum R. Ahn of the Department of Architecture and Architectural Engineering in collaboration with Prof. Jeongmi Lee's team at the Graduate School of Culture Technology at KAIST.

science.title: Mini-antibodies reactivate the 'guardian of the genome'
science.summary: Each year, 20 million people are diagnosed with cancer. Various organs can be affected, and cancer types sometimes differ greatly at the cellular and molecular level. In about half of all cases, however, the protein p53 is mutated. Known as the "guardian of the genome," it plays a central tumor suppressor role: In healthy cells, it ensures that DNA damage is detected and repaired. If this is not successful, the affected cell is selectively eliminated through apoptosis—an important protection against cancer. Conversely, cells can often only develop into tumor cells when the protein p53 is inactivated by a mutation. In many cases, it becomes unstable as a result of the mutation and loses its functional structure.

science.title: Marigold flowers show potential as a source of plant-based protein
science.summary: Our current fascination with high-protein foods means plant-based protein now shows up in many food products. But rather than growing plants just for their protein, researchers wondered if edible flowers—like pot marigolds that are usually composted or thrown away once they're past their prime—could be a new, sustainable plant-based protein source. So, they measured the protein content and composition of dried marigolds, and they report the results in ACS Food Science & Technology.

science.title: A Gently Glowing Galaxy
science.summary: A luminous swirl set against the deep black of space, the barred spiral galaxy IC 486 glows with a soft, ethereal light in this NASA Hubble Space Telescope image from April 13, 2026. IC 486 lies right on the edge of the constellation Gemini (the Twins), around 380 million light-years from Earth. Classified as a barred spiral […]

science.title: The 'tail' of the shrinking dog brain: Study reveals they began getting smaller 5,000 years ago
science.summary: Dogs have long been known to have smaller brains than the wolves they descended from. But when they started to shrink has been a matter of some debate. New research published in the journal Royal Society Open Science, which compared ancient and modern canid skulls, puts the date at around 5,000 years ago.

science.title: More than fashion: Uncovering the psychology behind the activewear trend
science.summary: Activewear has become an everyday staple for many Australian women, but new Edith Cowan University (ECU) research suggests it may be contributing to unexpected pressures around body image and appearance.

science.title: NASA, Boeing Advance Truss-Braced Wing Research in Test
science.summary: NASA and Boeing have completed wind tunnel testing to study an innovative advanced aircraft design intended to improve aerodynamic efficiency. A truss-braced wing configuration, involving a long, thin wing with aerodynamically shaped structural supports, has the potential to reduce fuel and operational costs for future airliners, which is why NASA has collaborated with Boeing to […]

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Actorul Cuba Gooding Jr., câștigător al Premiului Oscar®, a vizitat astăzi șantierul viitoarei Clinici de Psihiatrie Pediatrică „Prof. Dr. Alexandru Obregia”, alături de Codin Maticiuc, fondatorul inițiativei „Spitale Publice din… Articolul Actorul premiat cu Oscar Cuba Gooding Jr. vizitează șantierul noii Clinici de Psihiatrie Pediatrică de la Obregia și susține proiectul Fundației Metropolis apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 S-a aflat adevărata identitate a şoferului lui Simion. Era Grindeanu!
mix_items.summary: Mai ceva ca SPP-istul lui Nicuşor Dan, misteriosul şofer al liderului AUR George Simion a stârnit constant imaginaţia a mii de bărbaţi! Astăzi, însă, identitatea acestuia ieşit la iveală – şi, culmea, e de la un partid concurent. „În tot acest timp, şoferul lui Simion era Grindeanu! Oau! Mă încearcă nişte sentimente tare dubioase acum… […] The post S-a aflat adevărata identitate a şoferului lui Simion. Era Grindeanu! appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: 😂 Președinte-mediator! Grindeanu l-a trimis pe Nicușor să-i medieze un pachet de țigări
mix_items.summary: Om cu principii solide, Nicușor Dan rămâne un mediator indiferent de situația în care se regăsește. Chiar și azi, când Sorin Grindeanu l-a trimis după țigări, președintele și-a demonstrat încă o dată abilitățile de mediator și, în locul unui refuz categoric, a ales să-i medieze liderului PSD pachetul de țigări cerut. Nicușor Dan și-a mediat […] The post Președinte-mediator! Grindeanu l-a trimis pe Nicușor să-i medieze un pachet de țigări appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Why your brain turns against you during arguments, and what to do about it
mix_items.summary: My ex once told me, mid-argument, that I was the most unempathetic person he'd ever met. It was a low blow. I'm a clinical psychologist. Empathy is literally my job.

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
mix_items.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
mix_items.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

mix_items.title: International partners boost peace agreement success, study finds
mix_items.summary: Over the past 50 years, nearly 4 in 10 peace agreements have failed within five years of signing. New research shows that when international partners help implement an accord, the odds improve—and the deeper their engagement, the better.

mix_items.title: Observing exotic quasiparticle states in kagome superconductor CsV₃Sb₅
mix_items.summary: A research team led by Prof. Hao Ning of the Hefei Institutes of Physical Science of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, in collaboration with Anhui University and the University of Science and Technology of China, has identified two distinct types of unusual low-energy quasiparticle states in the kagome superconductor CsV3Sb5 using single-atom impurities as local "quantum probes" combined with scanning tunneling spectroscopy.

mix_items.title: Oxidative stress slows down the brain, but the brake can be released again
mix_items.summary: As we age, the brain undergoes various changes. Neurons function less efficiently, and key regulatory systems within the cells become unbalanced. Protein quality control, which ensures that proteins are properly processed, recycled, or broken down, is particularly affected.

mix_items.title: Designing in situ power stations for future Mars missions
mix_items.summary: You're in the lab analyzing Martian regolith samples within your cozy Mars habitat serving on the fifth human mission to Mars. The power within the habitat has been flowing flawlessly thanks to the MARS-MES (Mars Atmospheric Resource & Multimodal Energy System), including the general habitat lighting, science lab, sleeping quarters, exercise equipment, the virtual reality headsets the crew use for rest & relaxation, oxygen and fuel generation, and water. All this from converting the Martian atmosphere into workable electricity.

mix_items.title: Patients in low-income neighborhoods are less likely to receive cancer screenings
mix_items.summary: People living in areas with fewer resources are less likely to receive cancer screenings from federal clinics, according to a recent study from the University of Georgia published in the Journal of General Internal Medicine.

mix_items.title: Cities rethink beekeeping as honeybee boom may strain wild bees
mix_items.summary: The rising popularity of urban beekeeping has raised concerns about honeybee well-being and the impact they might have on wild bee populations in cities. A collaborative study by beekeepers, political stakeholders and research institutions, including researchers from the Technical University of Munich (TUM) has resulted in the "Urban Bee Concept," which includes measures to foster co-existence between honeybees and wild bees in cities. The study is published in the journal People and Nature.

mix_items.title: Choosing safer diabetes medications for older adults
mix_items.summary: Older adults with type 2 diabetes face a difficult trade-off: they are among the most vulnerable to medication-related harms yet are often underrepresented in the clinical trials that guide treatment decisions. A new study led by Yuan Lu, ScD, helps address this gap by providing large-scale, real-world evidence about the safety of commonly used diabetes medications. The study was co-led by Marc Suchard, Ph.D., of the University of California, Los Angeles, who also served as the study's corresponding author.

mix_items.title: Researchers detect microplastics in fish larvae shortly after hatching
mix_items.summary: Microplastics are now widely distributed throughout the environment—in water, in the air, in the soil and even inside living organisms, including marine life. However, most studies to date have focused on adult fish, including those used for human consumption. The history of these microplastics, however, remained unknown. It was not clear exactly when this contamination begins throughout the life cycle.

mix_items.title: Magic tricks can reduce stress, pain and anxiety for children during vaccinations
mix_items.summary: Injections can be a source of stress and anxiety for children. The Willem-Alexander Children's Hospital is exploring ways to improve the experience. Together with illusionist Victor Mids and researchers from Leiden, they have shown that magic can help. The work is published in the journal eClinicalMedicine.

mix_items.title: The most common planets in the galaxy don't appear around the most common stars, TESS observations suggest
mix_items.summary: Astronomers now estimate there is at least one planet for every star in our galaxy. These worlds, called exoplanets, are planets that orbit stars outside our solar system. But new research from McMaster University reveals a surprising twist: the most common planets in our galaxy don't exist around the most common stars.

mix_items.title: Researchers deepen understanding of molecular pathways responsible for airway inflammation in asthma
mix_items.summary: Johns Hopkins Medicine researchers say they have identified molecular pathways controlled by the protein RhoA in club cells—a specialized population of epithelial cells that exclusively lines the inner and outer surfaces of the lung's small airways, with known roles in immune regulation and airway repair—during allergen-induced airway inflammation. Through a series of translational mouse and cell culture experiments, the research team has demonstrated how RhoA can be selectively targeted to potentially prevent asthma-related complications.

mix_items.title: Common genetic marker may guide new treatment for acute leukemia
mix_items.summary: A genetic alteration that is already routinely analyzed in patients with acute myeloid leukemia can be used to identify patients who respond to a new targeted therapy, according to a study published in the journal Discover Oncology. Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is an aggressive blood cancer in which treatment outcomes vary widely between patients. In the new study, the researchers have identified a way to better select which patients may benefit from a drug that blocks the enzyme DCPS.

mix_items.title: Study reveals century-long cycles in US suicide rates and a long-term crisis among youth
mix_items.summary: Suicide rates in the United States follow striking, decades-long cycles likely shaped by broad social forces, according to a major new study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS). But beneath that long-term rise-and-fall pattern, researchers uncovered a deeply concerning and persistent trend: suicide risk among young people has been rising steadily for more than a half century, affecting each new generation earlier in life.

mix_items.title: Behavioral safety assessment may quantify the 'ability to see risk' to prevent industrial accidents
mix_items.summary: Seoul National University College of Engineering announced that a research team has developed a behavior-based safety assessment system capable of measuring construction workers' ability to perceive risks and respond appropriately to them. The team was led by Prof. Changbum R. Ahn of the Department of Architecture and Architectural Engineering in collaboration with Prof. Jeongmi Lee's team at the Graduate School of Culture Technology at KAIST.

mix_items.title: Mini-antibodies reactivate the 'guardian of the genome'
mix_items.summary: Each year, 20 million people are diagnosed with cancer. Various organs can be affected, and cancer types sometimes differ greatly at the cellular and molecular level. In about half of all cases, however, the protein p53 is mutated. Known as the "guardian of the genome," it plays a central tumor suppressor role: In healthy cells, it ensures that DNA damage is detected and repaired. If this is not successful, the affected cell is selectively eliminated through apoptosis—an important protection against cancer. Conversely, cells can often only develop into tumor cells when the protein p53 is inactivated by a mutation. In many cases, it becomes unstable as a result of the mutation and loses its functional structure.

mix_items.title: A routine virus can slow breast cancer spread to the lungs, offering hidden protective power
mix_items.summary: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), mostly infects the lungs, nose, throat, and respiratory tract, and can cause illness ranging from mild cold and fever-like symptoms to severe pneumonia and bronchitis. A recent study has found that having a respiratory infection can act as a shield against the spread of cancer cells.

mix_items.title: Marigold flowers show potential as a source of plant-based protein
mix_items.summary: Our current fascination with high-protein foods means plant-based protein now shows up in many food products. But rather than growing plants just for their protein, researchers wondered if edible flowers—like pot marigolds that are usually composted or thrown away once they're past their prime—could be a new, sustainable plant-based protein source. So, they measured the protein content and composition of dried marigolds, and they report the results in ACS Food Science & Technology.

mix_items.title: Phone-based education improves inhaler use in people with COPD
mix_items.summary: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), which includes emphysema and chronic bronchitis, affects more than 30 million Americans and is the fourth leading cause of death worldwide. Many people with COPD use inhaler-based medications as part of their treatment plan; however, research has shown that most people do not use these medications correctly, which can reduce effectiveness and result in more frequent exacerbations.

mix_items.title: A Gently Glowing Galaxy
mix_items.summary: A luminous swirl set against the deep black of space, the barred spiral galaxy IC 486 glows with a soft, ethereal light in this NASA Hubble Space Telescope image from April 13, 2026. IC 486 lies right on the edge of the constellation Gemini (the Twins), around 380 million light-years from Earth. Classified as a barred spiral […]

mix_items.title: The 'tail' of the shrinking dog brain: Study reveals they began getting smaller 5,000 years ago
mix_items.summary: Dogs have long been known to have smaller brains than the wolves they descended from. But when they started to shrink has been a matter of some debate. New research published in the journal Royal Society Open Science, which compared ancient and modern canid skulls, puts the date at around 5,000 years ago.

mix_items.title: More than fashion: Uncovering the psychology behind the activewear trend
mix_items.summary: Activewear has become an everyday staple for many Australian women, but new Edith Cowan University (ECU) research suggests it may be contributing to unexpected pressures around body image and appearance.

mix_items.title: Tiny daily movements key to preventing type 2 diabetes
mix_items.summary: Small habits like walking up flights of stairs or carrying your shopping instead of using a trolley could be the key to preventing Type 2 Diabetes, according to world-first research led by Monash University. In the first study in the world to highlight a link between short bursts of physical activity and lower risk of developing Type 2 Diabetes, published in Diabetes Care, researchers found less than four minutes a day of short bursts of moderate to intense activity could reduce a person's risk of developing the condition by more than a third.

mix_items.title: NASA, Boeing Advance Truss-Braced Wing Research in Test
mix_items.summary: NASA and Boeing have completed wind tunnel testing to study an innovative advanced aircraft design intended to improve aerodynamic efficiency. A truss-braced wing configuration, involving a long, thin wing with aerodynamically shaped structural supports, has the potential to reduce fuel and operational costs for future airliners, which is why NASA has collaborated with Boeing to […]

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why bread can cause weight gain without extra calories
mix_items.summary: Bread and other carbohydrate staples may be doing more than just filling plates—they could be quietly reshaping metabolism. In a surprising twist, researchers found that mice strongly preferred carbs like bread, rice, and wheat, abandoning their regular diet entirely. Even without eating more calories, they gained weight and body fat, not because they overate, but because their bodies burned less energy.

mix_items.title: Blocking a single protein supercharges the immune system against cancer
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered a way to supercharge the immune system’s T cells by blocking a protein called Ant2, forcing the cells to rewire how they generate energy. This shift makes them more powerful, resilient, and effective at finding and destroying cancer cells.

mix_items.title: Lonely people have worse memory but don’t decline faster, study finds
mix_items.summary: Loneliness may quietly affect how well older adults remember things—but it might not be speeding up mental decline after all. A large European study tracking over 10,000 people for seven years found that those who felt lonelier started off with weaker memory, yet their memory didn’t deteriorate any faster than those who felt more socially connected. The findings challenge the idea that loneliness directly accelerates cognitive decline or dementia, suggesting instead that it impacts baseline brain performance.

mix_items.title: This strange “pearling” motion inside cells could change how we understand disease
mix_items.summary: Mitochondria don’t just generate energy—they also carefully organize their own DNA in a surprisingly elegant way. Scientists have discovered that a long-overlooked phenomenon called “mitochondrial pearling,” where mitochondria briefly form bead-like shapes, helps evenly space clusters of mitochondrial DNA.

mix_items.title: The people you live with could be changing your gut bacteria
mix_items.summary: Spending time with close companions might do more than strengthen bonds—it could also reshape your gut bacteria. In a study of island birds, those with stronger social ties shared more gut microbes, especially types that require direct contact to spread. This suggests that social interaction itself—not just shared space—drives microbial exchange. The same process may be happening in human households through everyday closeness.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce plan ai pe azi? - Sa nu stric planul de ieri.

satire_ro.title: Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar
